Social Bonds

Pre-Agricultural Social Bonds

A Best Friend

Before farming, humans were already beside dogs. Archaeological discoveries prove that the relationship between Paleolithic hunters and gatherers was one of caring.

14,000-Year-Old Double-Burial

100 years ago, archeologists found a site in Germany called Bonn-Oberkassel where they discovered the fossils of a dog buried next to a man and woman. The dog bones showed it had a disease and was essentially useless for hunting, yet the humans spent time nursing it.

Scholarly Evidence:

"The Bonn-Oberkassel dog... suffered a morbillivirus (canine distemper) infection... Since canine distemper has a three-week disease course with very high mortality... survival without intensive human assistance would have been unlikely. Before and during this period, the dog cannot have held any utilitarian use to humans. We suggest that... humans... developed emotional and caring bonds for their dogs, as reflected by the survival of this dog..."

(Janssens Abstract)

My Research Findings:

Even before dogs were eating our crops or being bred for appearances, they were our family. This burial proves that there was a bond deeper than a functional relationship. It showed care in our history with dogs.
